A near-evergreen oak of the Southwest's sky-island foothills, with dark glossy leaves and sweet acorns (bellotas) prized as food. A keystone of the region's oak savannas.
The slow, rounded little pine of the Colorado Plateau, bearing the rich edible pine nuts central to Southwestern cuisine and Indigenous foodways. Supremely drought- and cold-hardy.
The giant columnar emblem of the Sonoran Desert, growing for over a century and crowning itself with white flowers (Arizona's state flower) and red fruit. A high-rise habitat for desert birds.
A stout barrel ribbed with hooked red spines, leaning south with age and ringed by orange-to-yellow flowers in late summer. Pineapple-scented fruit follows for wildlife.
The classic broad-padded prickly pear, with big yellow blooms and magenta fruit (tunas) eaten fresh or made into jelly. Forms sprawling thickets that shelter desert wildlife.
A deceptively fuzzy-looking cholla densely armored in straw-colored barbed spines that detach at a touch. Its dropped joints root to form spiny colonies across the open desert.
A clustering hedgehog cactus that opens large, vivid magenta cups among its spiny stems in spring, followed by edible strawberry-like fruit. A jewel of rocky desert slopes.
A tidy, ice-blue rosette of broad spine-tipped leaves that, after decades, sends up a towering candelabra of yellow flowers and then dies, leaving offsets. Supremely cold-hardy for an agave.
A tree-form yucca with a slender trunk topped by a fountain of grassy blades and tall spikes of creamy bells. Pollinated by the yucca moth and an icon of the Chihuahuan grasslands.
A striking silver-blue sphere of saw-toothed leaves (sotol) that throws up a tall, plumed flower stalk. Architecturally bold and tough, and the traditional source of the spirit sotol.
Not a true yucca but a grassy evergreen clump sending up arching coral-red flower wands for months that hummingbirds work tirelessly. One of the toughest, longest-blooming desert accents.
A fan of long, thorny, whip-like canes that leaf out within days of rain and tip themselves with flame-red flower clusters for hummingbirds. A living fence and a Sonoran signature.

The most drought-enduring shrub in North America and the defining plant of the warm deserts, scenting the air with 'rain' after a storm. Resinous evergreen leaves, yellow flowers, and clones that live thousands of years.
A silvery, mounding desert shrub that floats yellow daisies on long stalks above its felted leaves each spring. A reliable, fast bloomer that colors roadsides across the Southwest.
A leathery gray-green evergreen whose seeds yield the famous liquid wax 'jojoba oil.' Tough, long-lived, and an excellent waterwise hedge or screen.
A low, airy desert legume whose flowers are puffs of pink-red stamens like tiny powderpuffs, beloved by hummingbirds and bees. A nitrogen-fixer that blooms after winter rains.
A sprawling, nearly leafless desert shrub hung with tubular red flowers through the cool season, a winter lifeline for hummingbirds. Its name, chuparosa, means 'hummingbird' in Spanish.
A silvery evergreen (cenizo) that bursts into clouds of orchid-purple flowers a day or two after summer rains, earning it the name 'barometer bush.' Supremely heat- and drought-proof.
A silvery-stemmed western shrub that blazes gold in late summer when little else blooms, swarming with migrating butterflies and bees. A tough pioneer of dry, disturbed, alkaline ground.
A compact, nearly everblooming shrubby sage in jewel tones of red, coral, and pink that hummingbirds chase from spring to frost. Tidy, tough, and a mainstay of waterwise gardens.
Woolly silver foliage topped by bright yellow daisies on long stalks for most of the year, reseeding into cheerful drifts. One of the longest-blooming and easiest desert wildflowers.
An airy desert shrub bearing single white roses followed by feathery pink seed plumes that glow in the light. It blooms and fruits at once and stabilizes dry, rocky slopes.
A low, aromatic evergreen mound of needle-fine leaves that covers itself in golden daisies for much of the year. Resinous and deer-proof, perfect for hot rock gardens.
A grassy-looking evergreen forming a fountain of long, wiry, flexible leaves topped by a plume of tiny cream flowers. A soft, fine-textured anchor for dry, rocky desert gardens.
Tall wands of glowing rose-pink bells light up the Sonoran spring and draw early hummingbirds. A fast, free-seeding penstemon for desert gardens.
Brilliant scarlet tubular flowers march up tall stems in spring, built for hummingbird pollination. A tough, reliable penstemon for hot, dry, rocky ground across the Southwest.
A silvery desert mallow studded with apricot-orange cups through much of the year, a favorite of specialist desert bees. It thrives on heat, drought, and poor soil.
A neat, long-lived mound that covers itself for months in honey-scented white daisies with golden centers. One of the toughest and tidiest natives for hot, dry, rocky gardens.
Yellow daisies with maroon-backed rays that genuinely smell of chocolate, strongest in the cool morning air. A charming, long-blooming, drought-tough prairie-and-desert wildflower.
A sprawling mound that opens enormous, fragrant white trumpets at dusk for night-flying hawkmoths. Dramatic and easy, but every part is highly poisonous.
A low, spreading desert verbena smothered in rounded clusters of lavender flowers nearly year-round after rains. A tireless nectar source and quick groundcover for dry ground.
A fountain-shaped bunchgrass topped by airy, pink-tinged seed clouds, thriving in salty, alkaline, and seasonally wet desert flats where few grasses persist.
A stately blue-green bunchgrass sending up tall silvery-purple flower spikes in fall. A bold, elegant accent grass that shrugs off heat, drought, and clay.
A delicate bunchgrass whose long, reddish-purple awns catch the light like a shimmering haze, especially backlit. A pioneer that greens up and flowers after rain on poor, dry soils.
